project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve climate-resilient and safe transport access in the northwestern region of Honduras and promote sustainable livelihoods in selected areas through landscape management and women-led economic initiatives
project_overview.beneficiaries: 194,000 direct beneficiaries (51% women) in northwestern Honduras; catchment area could reach over 400,000 people including users from El Salvador

primary_locations: Departments of Cortés and Santa Bárbara
Municipalities: Quimistán, Azacualpa, Nueva Frontera, Macuelizo
Connection between CA-4 (near Macuelizo) and CA-13 (near Corinto)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Partial - Honduras faces high crime rates, weak institutional framework, and contextual risks related to illicit activities in the project area. The country is characterized as having structural fragilities with recurring cycles of growth and crisis. However, not classified as a fragile state per se.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Construction of the CA-4 - CA-13 Connection Road Corridor
key_components.description: Construction of a new 46-km, two-lane climate-resilient and safe road connecting CA-4 (near Macuelizo) and CA-13 (near Corinto). Includes enhanced drainage, slope protection, enhanced pavement, embankment protection, nature-based solutions, wildlife crossings, road safety features, and resettlement costs. Estimated land acquisition: 185 hectares.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Resilient improvement of feeder roads
key_components.description: Paving of approximately 41 km of existing unpaved feeder roads in four municipalities (Quimistán 16 km, Azacualpa 9 km, Nueva Frontera 7 km, Macuelizo 9 km) with climate-resilient and safe features. Includes drainage upgrades, slope stabilization, small bridges, and road safety measures. Supports Government's Municipal Interconnection Program.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Inclusive and integrated sustainable landscape management
key_components.description: Gender-inclusive sustainable landscape management and community-driven economic development in priority areas adjacent to Cusuco National Park. Activities include: restoration and reforestation of degraded landscapes, promotion of sustainable value chains and agroforestry, community-based ecotourism initiatives, and women's entrepreneurship. Excludes interventions within CNP.

key_components.component_name: Component 4: Institutional strengthening, capacity building, and project management
key_components.description: Subcomponent 4.1 (US$3M): Institutional capacity building for SIT including asset management, gender equality promotion, and citizen engagement. Subcomponent 4.2 (US$2M): Road safety management capacity enhancement. Subcomponent 4.3 (US$5M): Project management support including UEP staffing and consulting firm support.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Secretariat of Infrastructure and Transportation (SIT) through a Project Executing Unit (UEP). SIT maintains overall fiduciary responsibility. Municipalities and National Institute of Forest Conservation (ICF) provide technical and coordination support but do not directly manage credit funds.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Inter-institutional coordination mechanisms under SIT leadership. SIT will sign inter-institutional agreement with ICF for Component 3 implementation. Municipalities involved in Component 2 prioritization through participatory processes. Project Management and Monitoring Agency (consulting firm) to provide management support to SIT and UEP.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised September 2023). Procurement Plan covering first 18 months prepared. Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) developed with market analysis. Standard procurement documents used. Prior review for major contracts (Component 1 construction: international competitive bidding; Component 2 roads: national competitive bidding). Post-review for smaller contracts. Hands-on Expanded Implementation Support (HEIS) approved.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: UEP oversees M&E framework. Semi-annual reports within 30 days of reporting period. Mid-Term Review between 36-48 months after effectiveness. Annual reports with updated activity plans. Specialized M&E team within UEP. Regular Bank supervision with field visits. Implementation Completion Report within 4 months after project closure. Monitoring includes outcome and intermediate indicators, financial management, procurement, and adherence to social/environmental policies.

identified_risks.risk_description: Political situation in Honduras poses risks including possible delays in approval of external financing, changes in government priorities due to 2025 elections, and political uncertainty affecting institutional stability and project implementation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Ensure multi-sectoral support for the project; promote communication plan with key stakeholders to ensure continuity regardless of administrative changes

identified_risks.risk_description: Country faces high debt ratio and exposure to risk of default on bonds, which could compromise ability to take on new loans and prioritize debt repayment over infrastructure investments
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project aligns with GoH objectives of efficient fiscal resource use while maintaining essential infrastructure investment. Long-term project will help reduce default risk. Mitigation measures beyond project scope.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: SIT lacks experience managing World Bank-financed projects and has limitations in institutional capacity to implement new corridor to WB standards. Limited experience with externally funded projects.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Straightforward implementation arrangement with SIT as sole agency. SIT has stable, competent, experienced staff with advanced knowledge of resilient structures and road asset management. Dedicated UEP created within SIT. Specialized consulting firm hired for implementation support. Component 4 strengthens UEP capacity. Funding for UEP operational costs considered.

identified_risks.risk_description: SIT's lack of WB experience may lead to: failure to process required documentation for disbursements, delays in financial accounting/reporting, weak governance structure, and inaccurate cost estimations
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: FM fiduciary training for SIT and UEP staff; establishment of clear roles and responsibilities with transparent decision-making; thorough cost estimation exercises with FM staff involvement; regular monitoring system for cost estimates. Use of Honduras country systems (SIAFI/UEPEX). External audit by private firm following ISAs.

identified_risks.risk_description: SIT's limited experience with donor-funded projects and no recent WB experience; complex internal arrangements requiring multiple approvals; unfamiliarity with FIDIC contracts; uncertainty about international market participation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Competitively selected consulting firm with WB Procurement Regulations and FIDIC contract experience; streamlined internal SIT processes with clearly defined roles in POM; procurement training for SIT and UEP staff; targeted market analysis to identify potential providers; assessment and addressing of barriers for international and national bidders

identified_risks.risk_description: High environmental risk from 46-km greenfield road construction near Cusuco National Park (globally significant biodiversity area). Risks include habitat alteration/fragmentation, reduced wildlife mobility, impacts on archaeological remains, waste generation, drainage impacts, unsustainable material sourcing, dust/noise/vibration, wastewater generation, traffic disruptions, health/safety risks, natural hazard exposure, and potential indirect impacts from induced logging, agricultural expansion, and new settlements. Social risks include land acquisition (185 hectares), involuntary resettlement, labor influx (1,500 workers), SEA/SH risks, occupational/community health and safety, child labor, road safety, exclusion of vulnerable groups, illicit activities, and limited implementor capacity for ESF compliance.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Full ESIA during feasibility/design stage for Component 1 identifying mitigation measures. ESAs for Components 2 and 3. Labor Management Procedure (LMP), Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP), Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF), Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs), Indigenous Peoples Planning Framework (IPPF), and Environmental and Social Management Plans (ESMPs). Component 3 promotes conservation and community engagement. GBV Action Plan and Camp Management Plan for Component 1. Wildlife crossings (3 planned). Road safety audits at all stages. Community consultations held in August 2024. Grievance mechanism established. Gender-sensitive training and community awareness on GBV. Service provider mapping for SEA/SH. Worker codes of conduct.

identified_risks.risk_description: Multiple stakeholders with direct impact on implementation including communities benefiting from Components 1, 2, and 3 whose support and commitment is essential. Large list of key stakeholders creates complexity.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Stakeholder Engagement Plan with identification and mapping of key actors, information and consultation mechanisms, and grievance management mechanism. Community consultations conducted during preparation. Participatory process for Component 2 prioritization with focus on women's voice. Continuous monitoring and consultation throughout implementation. Citizen satisfaction surveys. Accessible and reliable grievance redress mechanism.

identified_risks.risk_description: Contextual risks related to existence of illicit activities in the project area. Honduras has high crime rates and weak institutional framework.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Community engagement and stakeholder consultation to build local support. Grievance mechanisms. Coordination with local authorities. Security assessments during implementation.

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: High Environmental and Social Risk Classification (ESRC High)
triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS 7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
OP/BP 7.50: Projects on International Waterways (Culupa, Nuevo Cacao, and Cholula rivers)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level grievance mechanism established. Accessible and reliable GRM to address concerns and complaints promptly and transparently with no cost or discrimination toward project-affected communities. Culturally appropriate grievance mechanisms in SEP and ESMPs. World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism also available.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender focus: Women represent 51% of catchment area population. Project aims to close gender gaps in transport sector employment and boost women's entrepreneurship. Specific activities include: (1) Women Engineering Trainee Program with internships at SIT (target 50 women); (2) Road safety auditing training and certification for women (target 30% of trainees); (3) Women-led initiatives for sustainable landscape restoration (target 80% women participation); (4) Community-based GBV prevention program integrated with economic empowerment; (5) Business and financial literacy training; (6) Identification of women-led entrepreneurships for community-based tourism and road maintenance microenterprises; (7) Women's employment opportunities in construction sector; (8) Gender-sensitive road design features improving safety for women and girls; (9) Participatory processes with focus on women's voice in Component 2 prioritization; (10) GBV Action Plan with risk mitigation measures including service provider mapping, worker codes of conduct, and training.
vulnerable_groups: Poor and low-income populations (70-89% poverty in primary beneficiary areas)
Indigenous Peoples (potential in expanded Component 2 areas)
Vulnerable road users (pedestrians, cyclists, motorized 2- and 3-wheelers)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project addresses conflict-related vulnerabilities indirectly through economic development and livelihood improvements. Honduras faces high crime rates and weak institutional framework. Project includes community engagement, grievance mechanisms, and stakeholder consultation to build local support and reduce tensions. Gender-focused activities and women's economic empowerment can contribute to social cohesion. However, project does not explicitly address conflict prevention or peacebuilding. Security risks from illicit activities in project area require careful management through community engagement and coordination with local authorities.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Honduras exhibits structural fragilities: (1) High poverty (51.3% below poverty line in 2023; rural poverty 64.1%); (2) Extreme vulnerability to natural disasters (most affected country globally 1996-2015; annual losses 2.1% GDP); (3) Weak institutional capacity and governance; (4) High crime rates and weak rule of law; (5) Limited infrastructure access in rural areas; (6) High unemployment and gender disparities; (7) Recurring cycles of growth and crisis; (8) Macroeconomic vulnerabilities (high debt, default risk); (9) Climate change exacerbating vulnerabilities. Project directly addresses infrastructure gap and climate resilience, contributing to reducing fragility through improved connectivity, economic opportunities, and institutional strengthening.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security risks include: (1) Contextual risks from illicit activities in project area; (2) Labor influx (1,500 workers) creating potential SEA/SH and community tensions; (3) Land acquisition and resettlement risks; (4) High crime environment in Honduras; (5) Weak institutional capacity for security management. Mitigation includes: community engagement and consultation, grievance mechanisms, GBV prevention measures, worker codes of conduct, community awareness programs, coordination with local authorities, and security assessments during implementation.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Involuntary resettlement expected: (1) Land acquisition of approximately 185 hectares for Component 1 corridor; (2) Possible restrictions on land use resulting in involuntary resettlement including physical and economic displacement; (3) Housing resettlement expected to be limited as alignment will not pass near or through communities; (4) Possible realignment and resettlement cases under Component 2 not expected unless under very limited circumstances. Mitigation: Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) and Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) to be prepared, disclosed, and adopted. RAPs required before resettlement costs disbursement. Climate-resilient considerations in resettlement design. Transparent, accountable, and appropriate use of funds ensured through FM assessment.
